content:
# Mark Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 40v–41r (PDF pages 81–82) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_015_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_015_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ At dawn, the chief priests called an urgent meeting with the elders, the scholars, and the full Sanhedrin. They bound Jesus, led him away, and handed him over to Pilate.

² Pilate asked him, "Are you the King of the Jews?" Jesus replied, "You say so."

³ The chief priests piled on accusation after accusation.

⁴ Pilate pressed him again: "Aren't you going to answer? Look how many charges they're bringing against you!"

⁵ But Jesus said nothing more, which astonished Pilate.

⁶ Now it was Pilate's custom during the festival to release one prisoner — whoever the crowd requested.

⁷ A man named Barabbas was in prison with some rebels who had committed murder during a recent uprising.

⁸ The crowd came and began asking Pilate to follow his usual custom.

⁹ "Do you want me to release the King of the Jews?" Pilate asked.

¹⁰ He could see that the chief priests had handed Jesus over out of jealousy.

¹¹ But the chief priests worked the crowd into demanding Barabbas instead.

¹² "Then what should I do with the one you call the King of the Jews?" Pilate asked.

¹³ "Crucify him!" they screamed.

¹⁴ "Why? What crime has he committed?" Pilate asked. But they just screamed louder: "Crucify him!"

¹⁵ Pilate, wanting to keep the crowd happy, released Barabbas. He had Jesus flogged and handed him over to be crucified.

¹⁶ The soldiers took Jesus inside the courtyard of the governor's headquarters and called out the entire regiment.

¹⁷ They dressed him in a purple robe, twisted together a crown of thorns, and jammed it on his head.

¹⁸ Then they began saluting him mockingly: "Hail, King of the Jews!"

¹⁹ They beat him on the head with a stick, spit on him, and knelt before him in fake worship.

²⁰ When they'd had their fun, they stripped off the purple robe, put his own clothes back on him, and led him out to crucify him.

²¹ They forced a man named Simon, from Cyrene — the father of Alexander and Rufus — who was passing by on his way in from the countryside, to carry the cross.

²² They brought Jesus to a place called Golgotha, which means "Skull Hill."

²³ They offered him wine drugged with myrrh, but he refused it.

²⁴ Then they nailed him to the cross. They divided up his clothes by gambling for them.

²⁵ It was nine in the morning when they crucified him.

²⁶ A sign was posted above him with the charge: THE KING OF THE JEWS.

²⁷ They crucified two criminals alongside him, one on his right and one on his left.

²⁸ [And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, "He was counted among the outlaws."]

²⁹ People passing by hurled insults at him, shaking their heads. "Ha! So you were going to destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days?

³⁰ Save yourself! Come down from the cross!"

³¹ The chief priests and scholars joined in the mockery: "He saved other people, but he can't save himself!

³² Let this 'Messiah,' this 'King of Israel,' come down from the cross right now — then we'll see it and believe!" Even the men crucified with him taunted him.

³³ At noon, darkness fell over the entire land and lasted until three in the afternoon.

³⁴ At three o'clock, Jesus cried out in a loud voice, "*Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?*" — which means, "My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?"

³⁵ Some of the bystanders heard and said, "Listen — he's calling for Elijah!"

³⁶ Someone ran, soaked a sponge in cheap wine, stuck it on a stick, and held it up to his lips. "Let's see if Elijah shows up to rescue him," the man said.

³⁷ Then Jesus let out a loud cry and died.

³⁸ At that moment, the curtain in the temple was ripped in two from top to bottom.

³⁹ The Roman centurion standing guard in front of Jesus, seeing how he died, said, "This man really was the Son of God."

⁴⁰ A number of women were watching from a distance, including Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the younger and Joses, and Salome.

⁴¹ These women had followed Jesus in Galilee and provided for him. Many other women who had come up with him to Jerusalem were also there.

⁴² That evening — it was Preparation Day, the day before the Sabbath —

⁴³ Joseph of Arimathea, a prominent member of the council who was himself waiting for God's kingdom, gathered his courage, went to Pilate, and asked for Jesus's body.

⁴⁴ Pilate was surprised that Jesus was already dead. He sent for the centurion and asked if it was true.

⁴⁵ When the centurion confirmed it, Pilate released the body to Joseph.

⁴⁶ Joseph bought a linen sheet, took the body down, wrapped it in the linen, and placed it in a tomb carved out of rock. Then he rolled a stone across the entrance.

⁴⁷ Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses watched carefully to see where he was laid.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Jesus's silence before Pilate (vv. 2-5):** Jesus says only three words to Pilate: "You say so." Then he falls silent. Mark's Jesus goes to his death without defending himself, fulfilling the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:7: "He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth."

2. **Barabbas — the bitter irony (vv. 6-15):** The name Bar-Abbas means "son of the father." The crowd chooses the "son of the father" (a murderer) over the actual Son of the Father. Mark may or may not intend this irony, but it's devastating either way.

3. **"My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?" (v. 34):** The only words Jesus speaks from the cross in Mark. This is Psalm 22:1. Some argue Jesus is reciting the entire psalm (which ends in vindication). But Mark gives no such comfort. In Mark, Jesus dies feeling abandoned. The theology of the cross is not softened.

4. **The centurion's confession (v. 39):** The great reversal. The disciples have fled. The women watch from afar. And it is a Roman executioner — a pagan, a Gentile — who speaks the truth that the entire Gospel has been building toward: "This man was the Son of God." Mark's Gospel opens with this title (1:1) and closes with it here. Everything between is the story of how the cross reveals what power conceals.

5. **The torn curtain (v. 38):** The moment Jesus dies, the temple curtain tears. God is no longer contained behind barriers. The old order is finished. Access to God is now through the cross, not the cult.

6. **The women (vv. 40-41, 47):** The male disciples have all vanished. Only the women remain — watching from a distance but watching. They are the thread of continuity between the crucifixion, burial, and resurrection. Mark names them carefully: they are his witnesses.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
